content:
{
  "title": "Execution-Aware Prompting: Integrating Partial Program Execution into LLM Code Generation Prompts",
  "problem": "Large language models often generate syntactically valid but semantically incorrect code due to insufficient grounding in runtime behavior. Existing prompting methods lack explicit mechanisms to incorporate feedback from partial code execution during inference time.",
  "motivation": "Even as LLMs become capable code generators, hallucination and semantic errors persist, particularly for complex logic and error handling. Incorporating runtime signal into the code generation loop could provide critical feedback to steer generation toward functionally correct solutions. Such integration is underexplored, and advances here could close the gap between generated and production-quality code.",
  "method": "We propose an execution-aware prompting strategy where the LLM is prompted with not only the task specification, but also the outputs and error traces from partial execution of its own intermediate code drafts. This is achieved by an iterative prompting loop: (1) the LLM generates a code draft, (2) an external sandbox executes this code on selected test cases, capturing outputs/errors, (3) the prompt is augmented with this feedback and resubmitted for code refinement. Unlike self-correction or self-revision approaches, execution feedback is injected directly into the prompt at each iteration, allowing the model to ground its reasoning in concrete program behavior.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS datasets. Baselines include standard zero-shot/few-shot prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, and self-revision methods. Metrics will include functional correctness (pass@k), syntactic validity, and error recovery rate. Ablation studies will analyze the impact of execution feedback granularity and iteration limits."
}
